tasks.title: Warm-up: Create Namespaces and a ServiceAccount
tasks.goal: Create two dedicated namespaces (staging, production) and a ServiceAccount, then use kubectl to directly observe the difference between the custom SA and the default SA.
tasks.prompt:
1. Create the staging and production namespaces imperatively.
2. Declare a ServiceAccount named qa in the staging namespace using a YAML manifest (qa-sa.yaml) and apply it.
3. Run kubectl get serviceaccount -n staging to verify that both the default SA and the qa SA appear.
4. Run kubectl describe serviceaccount qa -n staging and inspect the Mountable secrets and Tokens fields.
tasks.starter:
# 1. Create namespaces (imperative)
kubectl create namespace ____
kubectl create namespace ____

# 2. qa-sa.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: ____
  namespace: ____
tasks.solution:
# 1. Create namespaces
kubectl create namespace staging
kubectl create namespace production

# 2. qa-sa.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: qa
  namespace: staging

# Apply
kubectl apply -f qa-sa.yaml

# 3. List SAs
kubectl get serviceaccount -n staging
# Expected output:
# NAME      SECRETS   AGE
# default   0         10s
# qa        0         3s

# 4. Detailed inspection
kubectl describe serviceaccount qa -n staging
tasks.explanation: When a namespace is created, Kubernetes automatically creates a default ServiceAccount inside it. This default SA is the identity used by any Pod that does not specify a serviceAccountName. The reason we create a separate qa SA in this exercise is to isolate the permissions needed specifically by QA team Pods, in keeping with the principle of least privilege. Because ServiceAccounts are Kubernetes-native objects, they can be created and updated with kubectl apply -f just like any other resource.
hints: The kubectl create namespace command allows hyphens (-) but not underscores (_). If a name is rejected, check the character combination.
If you accidentally omit the namespace field in the YAML, the SA will be created in the default namespace. After applying, always confirm its location with kubectl get sa -n staging.

It is normal for the Tokens field to be empty in kubectl describe serviceaccount qa -n staging. Since Kubernetes 1.24, long-lived tokens are no longer automatically created when a ServiceAccount is created.

tasks.title: Core Exercise 1: Grant Least-Privilege with Role + RoleBinding, then Verify with can-i
tasks.goal: Create a Role that allows the qa SA to only read Pods and Services and access Pod logs within the staging namespace, bind it via a RoleBinding, and then verify both allow and deny cases with kubectl auth can-i --as.
tasks.prompt:
1. Write a Role named qa-read in the staging namespace (qa-role.yaml).
   - Allow get and list on pods and services
   - Allow get on pods/log
   - Do not grant any other verbs (create, delete, etc.).
2. Write a RoleBinding (qa-binding.yaml) that connects the qa SA to the qa-read Role and apply it.
3. Use kubectl auth can-i to check all four of the following cases, predict the expected output (yes/no) before running, then compare with the actual result.
   a. Can the qa SA get pods in staging?
   b. Can the qa SA delete pods in staging?
   c. Can the qa SA get pods/log in staging?
   d. Can the qa SA get pods in production?
tasks.starter:
# 1. qa-role.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: ____
  namespace: ____
rules:
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: [____, ____]
    verbs: [____, ____]
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: [____]
    verbs: [____]

# 2. qa-binding.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: qa-read-binding
  namespace: ____
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: ____
    namespace: ____
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: ____
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

# 3. can-i verification commands (fill in the blanks)
kubectl auth can-i get pods \
  --namespace ____ \
  --as system:serviceaccount:____:____

kubectl auth can-i delete pods \
  --namespace staging \
  --as system:serviceaccount:staging:qa

kubectl auth can-i get pods/log \
  --namespace staging \
  --as ____

kubectl auth can-i get pods \
  --namespace ____ \
  --as system:serviceaccount:staging:qa
tasks.solution:
# 1. qa-role.yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: qa-read
  namespace: staging
rules:
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["pods", "services"]
    verbs: ["get", "list"]
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["pods/log"]
    verbs: ["get"]

# 2. qa-binding.yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: qa-read-binding
  namespace: staging
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: qa
    namespace: staging
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: qa-read
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

# Apply
kubectl apply -f qa-role.yaml
kubectl apply -f qa-binding.yaml

# 3. can-i verification
# a. get pods in staging -> yes
kubectl auth can-i get pods \
  --namespace staging \
  --as system:serviceaccount:staging:qa
# Output: yes

# b. delete pods in staging -> no (delete not in rules)
kubectl auth can-i delete pods \
  --namespace staging \
  --as system:serviceaccount:staging:qa
# Output: no

# c. get pods/log in staging -> yes
kubectl auth can-i get pods/log \
  --namespace staging \
  --as system:serviceaccount:staging:qa
# Output: yes

# d. get pods in production -> no (RoleBinding only exists in staging)
kubectl auth can-i get pods \
  --namespace production \
  --as system:serviceaccount:staging:qa
# Output: no
tasks.explanation: RBAC is a 'deny by default' model. Only the verb and resource combinations explicitly listed in rules are allowed; everything else is denied. Because delete was not added to the rules in this exercise, (b) returns no. The reason (d) returns no is that the RoleBinding itself lives in the staging namespace — a RoleBinding grants permissions only within the namespace it belongs to. The --as system:serviceaccount:<namespace>:<name> format tells kubectl to impersonate the given SA and ask the API server for an authorization decision upfront. This allows you to verify permissions instantly without launching an actual Pod, making it extremely useful in practice.
hints: roleRef.kind must be either 'Role' or 'ClusterRole', and it is case-sensitive. Writing 'role' (lowercase) will cause an error when applying.
If a can-i result differs from your expectation, run kubectl describe rolebinding qa-read-binding -n staging first to confirm that subjects and roleRef are configured as intended.
The string format after --as is system:serviceaccount:<namespace>:<sa-name>. Omitting the colons (:) or the namespace will cause kubectl to treat it as a User identity and return unexpected results.

tasks.title: Core Exercise 2: Experience the Scope Difference Between ClusterRole + RoleBinding vs ClusterRoleBinding
tasks.goal: Demonstrate with can-i that binding the same ClusterRole via a RoleBinding restricts permissions to a specific namespace, while binding it via a ClusterRoleBinding grants permissions cluster-wide.
tasks.prompt:
1. Write a ClusterRole named pod-reader (pod-reader-cr.yaml) that allows get and list on pods and services.
2. Create a ServiceAccount named dev in the staging namespace.
3. Write a RoleBinding (dev-rb.yaml) in the staging namespace that connects the dev SA to the pod-reader ClusterRole.
4. Create a ServiceAccount named ops in the staging namespace.
5. Write a ClusterRoleBinding (ops-crb.yaml) that connects the ops SA to the pod-reader ClusterRole.
6. Verify the following four cases with can-i and explain the difference.
   a. Can the dev SA get pods in staging?
   b. Can the dev SA get pods in production?
   c. Can the ops SA get pods in staging?
   d. Can the ops SA get pods in production?
tasks.starter:
# 1. pod-reader-cr.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ____
metadata:
  name: pod-reader
  # no namespace field — ClusterRole is cluster-scoped
rules:
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["pods", "services"]
    verbs: ["get", "list"]

# 2. Create the dev SA
kubectl create serviceaccount dev -n staging

# 3. dev-rb.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: dev-rb
  namespace: ____
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: dev
    namespace: staging
roleRef:
  kind: ____     # referencing a ClusterRole
  name: pod-reader
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

# 4. Create the ops SA
kubectl create serviceaccount ops -n staging

# 5. ops-crb.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: ops-crb
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: ops
    namespace: staging
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: ____
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
tasks.solution:
# 1. pod-reader-cr.yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: pod-reader
rules:
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["pods", "services"]
    verbs: ["get", "list"]

# Apply
kubectl apply -f pod-reader-cr.yaml

# 2. Create dev SA
kubectl create serviceaccount dev -n staging

# 3. dev-rb.yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: dev-rb
  namespace: staging
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: dev
    namespace: staging
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: pod-reader
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

kubectl apply -f dev-rb.yaml

# 4. Create ops SA
kubectl create serviceaccount ops -n staging

# 5. ops-crb.yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: ops-crb
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: ops
    namespace: staging
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: pod-reader
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

kubectl apply -f ops-crb.yaml

# 6. can-i verification
# a. dev SA / staging -> yes (RoleBinding is in staging)
kubectl auth can-i get pods \
  --namespace staging \
  --as system:serviceaccount:staging:dev
# Output: yes

# b. dev SA / production -> no (RoleBinding only exists in staging)
kubectl auth can-i get pods \
  --namespace production \
  --as system:serviceaccount:staging:dev
# Output: no

# c. ops SA / staging -> yes
kubectl auth can-i get pods \
  --namespace staging \
  --as system:serviceaccount:staging:ops
# Output: yes

# d. ops SA / production -> yes (ClusterRoleBinding is cluster-wide)
kubectl auth can-i get pods \
  --namespace production \
  --as system:serviceaccount:staging:ops
# Output: yes
tasks.explanation: The dev SA and ops SA both reference the same ClusterRole (pod-reader), but the type of Binding gives them completely different permission scopes. Because dev SA is connected via a RoleBinding, permissions are valid only within staging — the namespace the RoleBinding belongs to. Even though the ClusterRole is cluster-wide, the RoleBinding narrows the scope to staging. The ops SA, on the other hand, is connected via a ClusterRoleBinding, so permissions apply cluster-wide regardless of namespace. This difference is exactly why you can define a single common ClusterRole and let each team attach their own RoleBinding in their own namespace — granting equivalent permissions without ever duplicating the permission definition.
hints: When referencing a ClusterRole in a RoleBinding, roleRef.kind must be 'ClusterRole'. Writing 'Role' will make Kubernetes look for a namespace-scoped Role with that name and return a 'not found' error.
ClusterRoleBinding has no metadata.namespace field. Adding one by mistake does not break anything (Kubernetes ignores it), but it is conventional to omit it to avoid confusion.
If (d) does not return yes for the ops SA, run kubectl describe clusterrolebinding ops-crb to confirm that subjects[0].name is 'ops' and namespace is 'staging'.

tasks.title: Advanced: Assign a ServiceAccount to a Pod and Disable automountServiceAccountToken
tasks.goal: Confirm that assigning a specific ServiceAccount to a Pod allows it to call the API under that SA's permissions, then practice disabling automountServiceAccountToken: false on Pods that don't need API access to reduce the attack surface.
tasks.prompt:
1. Write a Pod manifest (qa-pod.yaml) in the staging namespace that specifies qa as the serviceAccountName and apply it.
   - Image: bitnami/kubectl:latest (a kubectl-equipped image)
   - Command: sleep 3600
2. Use kubectl exec to enter the Pod and perform the following:
   a. Run ls /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/ to confirm the token file is mounted.
   b. Run kubectl get pods -n staging to query the Pod list under the qa SA's permissions.
   c. Run kubectl delete pod qa-pod -n staging to verify the delete request is denied.
3. Write a second Pod manifest (no-token-pod.yaml) that adds automountServiceAccountToken: false and apply it.
4. Use kubectl exec inside no-token-pod to run ls /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/ and confirm the token is not mounted.
5. Use can-i to make a final verification that the delete pods permission is no for the SA attached to both Pods.
tasks.starter:
# 1. qa-pod.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: qa-pod
  namespace: staging
spec:
  serviceAccountName: ____
  containers:
    - name: kubectl
      image: bitnami/kubectl:latest
      command: ["sleep", "3600"]

# 3. no-token-pod.yaml (fill in the blanks)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: no-token-pod
  namespace: staging
spec:
  serviceAccountName: qa
  ____: false     # disable automatic token mounting
  containers:
    - name: kubectl
      image: bitnami/kubectl:latest
      command: ["sleep", "3600"]
tasks.solution:
# 1. qa-pod.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: qa-pod
  namespace: staging
spec:
  serviceAccountName: qa
  containers:
    - name: kubectl
      image: bitnami/kubectl:latest
      command: ["sleep", "3600"]

kubectl apply -f qa-pod.yaml
kubectl get pod qa-pod -n staging   # wait for Running

# 2a. Confirm token is mounted
kubectl exec -n staging qa-pod -- ls /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/
# Expected output: ca.crt  namespace  token

# 2b. Query Pod list under qa SA permissions (allowed)
kubectl exec -n staging qa-pod -- kubectl get pods -n staging
# Expected output: current Pod list in staging (including qa-pod)

# 2c. Attempt deletion (verify it is denied)
kubectl exec -n staging qa-pod -- kubectl delete pod qa-pod -n staging
# Expected output: Error from server (Forbidden): pods "qa-pod" is forbidden:
#   User "system:serviceaccount:staging:qa" cannot delete resource "pods" ...

# 3. no-token-pod.yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: no-token-pod
  namespace: staging
spec:
  serviceAccountName: qa
  automountServiceAccountToken: false
  containers:
    - name: kubectl
      image: bitnami/kubectl:latest
      command: ["sleep", "3600"]

kubectl apply -f no-token-pod.yaml

# 4. Confirm token is not mounted
kubectl exec -n staging no-token-pod -- ls /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/
# Expected output: ls: cannot access '/var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/': No such file or directory
# (the token directory itself does not exist)

# 5. Final can-i verification
kubectl auth can-i delete pods \
  --namespace staging \
  --as system:serviceaccount:staging:qa
# Output: no
tasks.explanation: When serviceAccountName is specified in a Pod, Kubernetes automatically mounts the SA's token at /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token. Code running inside the container can include this token in an HTTP Authorization: Bearer <token> header to call kube-apiserver within the permission bounds granted to that SA. In step 2b, get pods succeeds; in step 2c, delete pods returns Forbidden — because the qa-read Role includes only get and list, not delete. Setting automountServiceAccountToken: false prevents the token file from being mounted at all. If the container is ever compromised, an attacker cannot obtain the kube-apiserver token. For applications that never need to call the Kubernetes API (simple web servers, batch jobs, etc.), applying this setting as a default is a security best practice.
hints: Pulling the bitnami/kubectl image may take some time. Watch the status with kubectl get pod qa-pod -n staging -w and wait until it shows Running before running exec.

If you see 'Error from server: the server doesn't have a resource type "pods"' in step 2b, the kubectl inside the Pod cannot locate the kube-apiserver address. Confirm that KUBERNETES_SERVICE_HOST is set by running kubectl exec -n staging qa-pod -- env | grep KUBERNETES.

In step 4, receiving a 'No such file or directory' error is the expected correct outcome. If the directory still exists, check that the automountServiceAccountToken field is placed directly under spec (not under containers) by reviewing the YAML indentation.
